sentences.text: I was starting to tell you before, but we never sort of got to any conclusion there, that my very early reaction, right, against the-- against the restrictive measures which came into force and the-- and the adverse propaganda which always accompanied that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the reaction I got out of that that I started to query myself, right?
I almost started to give the benefit of the doubt to them, right.
But that, as I said earlier, it didn't last long.
Because I realized that they are wrong, and we are right.
During this time, I sought the company of non-Jewish friends, which I had, quite a few.
Well, [SIGHS] look, I was in a-- in a tremendous quandary at that time, I-- if I remember well.
Because, because I-- being a Hungarian-- I felt that I belonged to-- there.
And they accepted me because they needed me to recite their poems.
They needed me to kick their goals.
And it, it was no problem to, to be with them.
And I liked that.
I liked that, the Jewish boy being there, accepted and, and, and appreciated, right?
And that was, I think, at the expense of my, my, my Jewish connections, right?
Of course, I, I never denied that I was a Jew.
I always went to the synagogue, and I did everything else.
But in my social life, perhaps, I had this conscious orientation for a year, a couple of years or so.
As, however, things deteriorated, I-- and I, I, I went through this metamorphosis-- I got my back up.
And I-- and the reaction set in by saying, to hell with you.
I'm Jewish.
I will keep-- I will keep reciting your poems, of course.
And I will kick as many goals as I can.
However, I am Jewish.
And I belong to, to that side.
And, and I have more evenly started to distribute my-- my time, my social orientations.
And that's when we formed a little club, which, incidentally at the time, had it been officially known, would have been a no-no.
Because there's no socially-- no organization clubs were possible in those times for political reasons, anti-establishment reasons, and of course, Jewish reasons.
But we had our little social clubs which consisted of eight boys from the class, which happened to be all Jewish.
And that's where my social life from the age of 16, 17, was, was concentrated on.
Oh, of course I do.
I remember all eight of them.
Would, would you like to know?
Well, I know all eight of them.
The eight boys involved the-- out of the eight boys, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Fodor Laci, he died during the war.
He was in Bor.
He was in, in, in-- you-- have you heard of the labor camp in Bor, B-O-R--
--in-- in Yugoslavia?
It was a very famous, or infamous, labor camp.
He died there.
Bern Micky was with with, who is now in Los Angeles, whom I regularly visit if I'm there.
Three are in Israel-- Orany Piszta, Polag Sanyi, and Weiner Dzuri.
Very interesting that these boys, whom I also visit when I go there-- last time, we had a very interesting meeting in which we more or less, you know, drew balances of our lives, what we have done.
And they-- obviously they went there because they're ardent Zionists.
However, they have their share of disappointments, which I was personally very sorry to see.
I would have been much happier if I have seen that they are completely relaxed in their decision.
[SIGHS] Well, I wasn't a Zionist.
I would like to--
I, I would like to put this on record.
Apparently, in my case, the gravity towards being a Hungarian must have been too, too, too strong for me to become a Zionist.
I will talk to, to you about that in a very interesting context later on.
Because when I obtained my false papers later on in '44, I got it through the Zionists, through these people, my early, early false papers.
And because I wasn't a member of the organization and because they are being a very pragmatic, even-handed lot, I had to-- had to do things for it, you know.
You didn't earn it just because you happen to be a classmate, a Jew.
You were not a Zionist.
You haven't been toeing the line the previous two, three years as they have.
Now I understand, right.
So they were the early Zionists, right, the three.
And I'm coming back to the eight people what--
--I, I counted four.
One died, one in Los Angeles, three in Israel.
I am here.
And [NON-ENGLISH] is also here, strangely.
And Fodor Laci died in war, right.
So that's eight people.
So out of the eight, we haven't been doing too badly.
Only one died-- very, very good statistics.
There are three in Israel, one in Los Angeles, two here, and-- that's eight, isn't it?
Right.
So this group of eight formed a strong part of your social life--
Yes.
Oh, of course.
Oh, very much so.
Very much so.
I mean, the eight-- the eight boys, you know, we went out, and we had parties.
And parties-- of course, girls were very strong part of our early interest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Most of them were Jewish girls.
And there were some non-Jewish girls involved.
No non-Jewish boys involved in that-- in that group.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But as matters became by law-- because the second Jewish law and the Jewish law came in, regulated.
Social intercourse between Jews and, and non-Jews have been not only discouraged, but, but, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but strictly inhibited by-- by law.
Right.
So much so that you couldn't marry a non-Jewish person.
You couldn't have sex with a non-Jewish person.
That was all strictly and, and severely punishable, right.
So in this atmosphere, our social activity was completely restricted within Jewish people.
[SIGHS] So when I matriculated in school, because of the numerous clauses, nothing was, was open for me.
And my father, who was essentially a very practical person, said, well, you have to learn a trade.
So we looked around.
It wasn't very easy at the time even to get an apprenticeship for a Jewish boy because a lot of Jewish boys were out and trying to-- to do something.
We somehow managed-- pardon me-- to get a-- pardon me-- to get to an apprenticeship in a-- in a toolmaking workshop, where I have been committed for one-and-half-year apprenticeship.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now I would like to say this.
I'm not a particularly mechanically-minded person, never been.
I always had some ability and some inclination to follow plans and, and be-- tried to be accurate, even in manual works-- work as well.
This one and a half year was a very hard time of my life.

It was hard because, A, I wasn't particularly cut out to be a toolmaker, although I, I made my living out of it for quite a number of years in Belgium and here, to which we are going to come later on.
But not so much my, my ineptitude to, to be a toolmaker, but the fact that we have been utterly exploited.
I have been utterly exploited.
I've been cleaning toilets.
I've been cleaning blacksmith setups.
At best, I've been doing repetitious work, drilling all day, right, which certainly didn't occupy my, my-- satisfy my intellectual [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] requirements.
And I learned some toolmaking, I suppose.
I have learned how, how to-- how to make things, but-- because I didn't have this born-with ability and because I wasn't given a great deal of opportunity, really.
What was interesting, however, that amongst the people I worked with, it-- there were, let's say, three typical people, right?
It gives you a very good background to the Hungary of that time.
It was the old [HUNGARIAN].
[HUNGARIAN] is Uncle [NON-ENGLISH].
Who was a man-- at the time, he would have been about, oh, 55.
He was a very old man, we thought-- I certainly thought.
And he was a, a leftist.
He was one of the few Hungarian early socialists who, who has been strongly absorbed in the socialistic principles.
And he was, at that time, which was very dangerous, voicing that.
And everybody knew.
And everybody made fun out of him.
And he was lucky that nothing else happened, right?
Rehaoul ?], was my, my, my superior.
Rehau ?] is a Slav name.
He must be of some Slav background, who was a typical tradesman of the time.
He certainly-- he certainly exerted his authority over me.
And he only started to give some respect to me when he realized that I cleaned all the toilets, and I drilled all the holes.
So, you know, it's not-- no more torture is available.
So that's when he started to teach me things about toolmaking.
And he became quite, quite human, quite-- quite good.
I had an interesting encounter with him during the last time, which I would like to tell you later on.
There was another, younger toolmaker.
His name was [?
Abarman, ?] [?
Abarman Laci. ?] He was of German origin, as the name itself would indicate.
And now, he was a, a typical product of the times.
He had some education.
And I imagine, if he had better background, he would have had even better education because he had the brain-- brains for it.
He was the one who was ambitious.
He was the one who realized that I would be of help to him.
Because when he had more complicated issues to, to make, to have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] numbers and certain educational background to apply, he came to me.
And I was very appreciative.
I, I helped him.
And it gave me a certain status and helped him because he didn't know what cosine is and, and, and, and all this trigonometry was, right, et cetera.
And I was-- that was very fresh in my, my academic mind at the time.
And I helped him.
And it was quite a good relationship.
I never really trusted him.
However, we were convenient for each other.
So after one and a half year-- after one and a half year, I had my-- I, I finished my apprenticeship.
And I got my papers as a-- as a toolmaker.
It was connected with a formal examination during which it was theory as well as practical examination, which I did reasonably well.
So I became a toolmaker in March, 1944.
Now this is well into the progress of the war.
Yes.
What sort of information received?
Well, you must understand one thing, that, you know, we are not living in a-- in a-- in a-- in a free-- in a free communication era, right?
The information we received was strictly censored [LAUGHS] and strictly directed and strictly under the, the final instruction of Goebbels, coming from as high up as that, right?
[SIGHS] Look, gossip-- it was a very-- was a very difficult time for-- time, time for us.
Because in 1940, '41, up to '42 when the war started to turn in Stalingrad-- I remember from history that.
Hitler was extremely successful-- extremely successful.
Hitler got Austria, the Sudetenland.
He had France in 1940, walked through-- around the Maginot Line in five minutes.
He got Holland.
He got, got Belgium, Dunkirchen, Dunkirk.
Remember that?
Came-- Mhm.
You know what Dunkirchen is?
The English--
Dunkirk, yes, landed in, in Western Europe, right?
It was a-- it was a malconceived landing.
And the Germans beat the daylight out of them.
And poor fellows, they were standing in the-- in the water there, waiting for a ship to pick them up.
And they died by the thousands, right?
It was a-- it was a humiliating defeat for the English at the time.
No, well, by the time, I, I, I very clearly saw where I belonged to.
I mean-- I mean-- if-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] impressionable I was, but foolish I wasn't, right?
By that time, I knew very clearly where I belonged to.
However, your, your question was, what was my early concept of, of Nazis?
Well, I, I, I, I-- even as a young man, I have been a very realistic person.
And I remember, I had arguments with my uncles, right, which was completely unconventional.
Because they have been foolhardy-- because they have been unduly optimistic.
And they had this easy attitude, well, everything will be all right.
And I, I, I saw-- I knew that everything will not be all right.
And by that time, I was helpless.
It's nothing I could do-- absolutely nothing.
And that is very difficult for you people to understand why, you know, so many people went to, to the abattoir, you know, without actually resisting and proving and trying to do something about it.
I mean, we are still at the early stages.
We are going to come to the-- to the-- you know, when, when, when life was really in danger.
At this stage, you know, it was no life danger yet.
We are in 1942, right?

[SIGHS] And, and the, the, [LAUGHS] the propaganda coming from the radio-- we didn't have television-- from the papers, from the pulpit, from everywhere, was so misguided and it was so concentrated, right, that it was obviously-- what Hitler was, was determined to do is to, to, to reduce us to a-- to a, a status of the cockroach, of a-- of a vermin, right?
And to kill a cockroach, there is no problem.
You put your foot on that cockroach, and you don't have any bad conscience, right?
And that was the atmosphere towards which the propaganda was building up to-- and very successfully, I must, must admit.
And aside from the impressions it gave to the non-Jews about how to look at Jews, how did it make Jews personally feel?
Was it-- was there resentment or submission?
Was there-- and when I say that, I mean really in your family.
What were the reactions to this barrage of propaganda-- of your father, of your mother?
Submission.
It was-- it was definitely no, no organized resistance of any type.
It was no opportunity to do that, right.
It was simply-- if you are in, in, in straitjacket, you cannot hit back, right?
And we were in straitjacket.
I mean--
[SIGHS] Look, my-- my poor father, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] towards-- you know, in '43, '44, he was, by my measure, too, he was a relatively young man.
He was 59 at that time, not even 60.
They took his commercial license away.
His activity was reduced to nil.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
We didn't have particularly strong financial resources and, and accumulated assets to live on.
So that was a constant worry, of course.
And any-- but, but psychologically, he was a broken man, right?
I remember him as sitting in a-- in a-- in an armchair like that and reading the paper.
He was smoking cigarettes quite heavy-- smoking cigarettes and falling asleep, right?
And he was of no, no value as, as, as, as a-- as giving guidance to us by that time.
My mother, who had considerably more spirit, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] did not contribute in a-- in a-- in a-- in a-- tangible way.
But always by, by, by her remarks, by her philosophy, by implied philosophy, always provided more, more hope, more, more, more sunshine.
However, however, we knew that we are doomed.
And it was a horrible situation because we knew that there's not much we can do about it.
So what is-- what was happening, right, I mean, you must know the, the, the political and military constellation of the time.
Hitler, as we were saying earlier, was, was winning, right.
He was successful, increasingly so, until the Russians stopped them in Stalingrad.
The Paulus Army, you know, suffered a tremendous setback.
Hundreds of Germans had been taken as prisoners.
And we didn't know that.
We know it now, right.
We sensed it then.
That-- because the, the, the official-- the official notification wasn't as clear as today.
You didn't have the CNN on the-- what's her name on the spot there and providing you with on-the-spot information.
It was a highly, highly artificial information, whatever the censor managed to get through.
And how it was worded, had hardly any, any connection with the actual event.
But you could read between the lines.
And you realized that they have gone through Stalingrad.
Indeed, they haven't even occupied Stalingrad, you know.
We knew that.
And we sensed that there is going to be a, a turnaround.
Oh, the fear.
Now, now, by the time-- by the time we have realized-- you see, [SIGHS] human beings are always-- are always hoping, always optimistic, right.
We always have been-- and we're, we're part of that.
We have been always hoping that somehow it would not happen to us, right.
We knew what happened in Slovakia, because by that time-- I had relatives who came from Slovakia, like, like, like, like, wild animals, right, who have been dislodged from their habitat, right?
And it's very interesting what our reaction to that was when that fellow-- who was a second cousin to my mother-- knocked on our door and told us those horrible stories in 1942, right?
What did he tell you?
Pardon?
Well, well, I mean-- as you know, they went through in 1942 what we went through in 1944 later on.
They were put in a cattle wagon and taken away, right, essentially.
Well, he, he illegally crossed the Hungarian border and came to Hungary.
And he knocked on our door.
And I remember our reaction very, very clearly.
We listened to him with almost disbelief, right.
And we realized we have to help the man, and we did.
And he slept there for awhile.
But then again, the, the official decrees appeared because he was not the only one.
And between you and I, we were very relieved when he eventually moved on after a few days, right?
Because it was illegal to, you know, to hide illegal refugees of this type.
Where did he go?
Really, I'm not sure.
I'm not sure.
I think he eventually found his way to Israel.
But we, you know-- people are very egocentric human beings, right?
We satisfied our, our obligation by helping him temporarily.
We gave him some money.
And the fact is that we were relieved when he moved on.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was no, no organized-- it's very hard to understand for you.
Because nowadays, such a thing cannot happen, thank God.
But in that atmosphere, it was no room for, for a great deal of organization.
The only people who had the resemblance of, of doing something in an organized way were the Zionists.
They have in a modest way.
And it resulted in salvaging a few people-- a few hundred people.
They crossed the border at Romania-- and I think that's what this man did-- came through Romania and Yugoslavia.
Eventually, they found their way to Palestine, as it was called at that time.

But that was the only organized [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] deviation from the ordinary, the meek ordinary, apart from some individual effort, which I have done later on, which I'm going to tell you about.
But that was not organized.
That was a--
--individual, absolutely single effort.
[SIGHS] So [SIGHS] in-- so in '44, in '44, I, I, I obtained my, my certificate of being a fully fledged toolmaker.
The major event was in January.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] January 18, 1944, my poor mother had a stroke.
I remember I was called back from my toolmaking thing because a phone call came through.
And he-- she was 49 at that time.
And when I got home, I realized that she is stricken.
And from January 1944 until, until March '44, she was in a hospital.
She was a young woman.
She was only 44.
And this embolia, this clot which was lodged in her brain, was slowly dissolving to the extent that she regained perhaps 80% of her movements.
She regained 75% of her mental faculties.
But she was a, a, a, a sick woman.
And she came back from, from the hospital in March.
On the 19th of March, 1944, the same, same time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Germans came in.
The Germans occupied Hungary.
By that time-- the book I have given to you describes this area-- this era very, very well.
By that time, the, the leaders of Hungary have realized that the Germans seem to have lost the war.
By that time, the Russians were pushing them back from the east.
The Allies were about to land from the west.
They have been pushed out from North America.
They have been pushed through Italy, you know, the, the Patton Army has been-- pushed the Germans out of Italy.
By that time--
By that time, Italy, as the major supporter of the Axis, has been-- has opted out, right?
So things have changed.
But we realized that Hitler has got enough stranglehold on, on, on satellite countries like Hungary that we are far from being out of danger.
OK, Mr.
Angles, just leave it there for the moment.
We'll change--
